pages.title: Application to the fundamental group
pages.html:
X Application to the fundamental group

Throughout this exposé, we denote by \(X\) a locally noetherian prescheme, by \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\), by \(U\) a variable open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), and by \(\widehat{X}\) the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\) (EGA I 10.8). For any prescheme \(Z\), we denote by \(\mathbf{Et}(Z)\) the category of étale coverings of \(Z\), and by \(\mathbf{L}(Z)\) the category of locally free coherent Modules on \(Z\).

3mm

pages.title: Comparison of \(\mathbf{Et}(\widehat{X})\) and \(\mathbf{Et}(Y)\)
pages.html:
1 Comparison of \(\mathbf{Et}(\widehat{X})\) and \(\mathbf{Et}(Y)\)

Let \(\mathscr{I}\) be an ideal of definition of \(Y\) in \(X\). Set, for every \(n\in\mathbf{N}\), \(Y_n=(Y, (\mathscr{O}_X/\mathscr{I}^{n+1}){|Y})\). The \(Y_n\) form an inductive system of ordinary preschemes, or also of formal preschemes, by endowing the structure sheaves with the discrete topology. It is known (EGA I 10.6.2) that \(\widehat{X}\) is the inductive limit, in the category of formal preschemes, of the inductive system of the \(Y_n\). It is also known (EGA I 10.13) that to give a formal \(\widehat{X}\)-prescheme of finite type \(R\) is to give an inductive system of ordinary \(Y_n\)-preschemes \(R_n\) of finite type, such that \(R_n\simeq(R_{n+1})\times_{(Y_{n+1})}(Y_n)\). Moreover, for \(R\) to be an étale covering of \(\widehat{X}\), it is necessary and sufficient that for every \(n\), \(R_n\) be an étale covering of \(Y_n\). This said, it is easy to see that nilpotent elements do not count for étale coverings (SGA I 8.3), that is to say that the base-change functor:

\[ \mathbf{Et}(Y_{n+1}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Et}(Y_n) \]

is an equivalence of categories for every \(n\in\mathbf{N}\). Hence:

Proposition 1.1

With the notations introduced above, the natural functor \(\mathbf{Et}(\widehat{X})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(Y)\) is an equivalence of categories (cf. SGA I 8.4).

pages.title: Comparison of \(\mathbf{Et}(Y)\) and \(\mathbf{Et}(U)\), for \(U\) variable
pages.html:
2 Comparison of \(\mathbf{Et}(Y)\) and \(\mathbf{Et}(U)\), for \(U\) variable

We are going to introduce two conditions from which the announced comparison theorem will follow easily. Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). One says that the pair \((X, Y)\) satisfies the Lefschetz condition, which one writes \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\), if, for every open set \(U\) of \(X\) containing \(Y\) and every locally free coherent sheaf \(E\) on \(U\), the natural homomorphism

\[ \Gamma(U, E) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(\widehat{X}, \widehat{E}) \]

is an isomorphism.

One says that the pair \((X, Y)\) satisfies the effective Lefschetz condition, which one writes \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), if one has \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\) and if moreover, for every locally free coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{E}\) on \(\widehat{X}\), there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) and a locally free coherent sheaf \(E\) on \(U\) and an isomorphism \(\widehat{E}\simeq\mathscr{E}\).

These conditions are satisfied in two important examples:

Example 2.1

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring and let \(t\in \mathfrak{r}(A)\) be an \(A\)-regular element belonging to the radical \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) of \(A\). Suppose that \(A\) is a quotient of a regular local ring and that \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology (for example \(A\) complete for the \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\)-adic topology). Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(Y'=V(t)\), and set moreover \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\) and \(X=X'-\{x\}\), \(Y=Y'-\{x\}\). Thus \(X\) is open in \(X'\) and \(Y=X\cap Y'\). Then:

  1. If, for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p}=1\) (i.e. for every closed point of \(X\)) one has \(\operatorname{prof} A_{\mathfrak{p}}\geqslant 2\), then one has \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\);

  2. if moreover, for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) such that \(t\in\mathfrak{p}\) and \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p}=1\) (i.e. for every closed point of \(Y\)), one has \(\operatorname{prof} A_{\mathfrak{p}}\geqslant 3\), then one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\).

Let us first show that for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\), the complement of \(U\) in \(X\) is a union of a finite number of closed points (in \(X\)). Note that \(U\) is open in \(X\), hence in \(X'\), so \(Z'=X'-U\) is closed. Let \(I\) be an ideal of definition of \(Z'\); it suffices to prove that \(A/I\) is of dimension \(1\). Now \(Z'\cap Y'=\{x\}\) so \(A/(I+(t))\) is artinian, whence the conclusion thanks to the "Hauptidealsatz".

The first hypothesis is equivalent to: "for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\), \(\mathfrak{p}\neq \mathfrak{r}(A)\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} A_\mathfrak{p}\geqslant3-\dim A/\mathfrak{p}\)". Indeed \(A\) is a quotient of a regular ring, so one can apply VIII 2.2 to the prescheme \(X'\), to the closed subset \(\{x\}\) and to the coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\).

Let \(U\) be an open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\) and \(E\) a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-module. Set \(Z=X-U\) and let \(u\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical immersion. We shall first prove that \(u_\ast(E)\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, or, what amounts to the same, that \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Z(E')\) is coherent for \(i=0, 1\), where \(E'\) is a coherent extension of \(E\) to \(X\). For this one applies theorem VIII 2.1 to the prescheme \(X\), to the closed subset \(Z\) and to the coherent sheaf \(E'\). It suffices to verify that for every point \(p\in U\) such that \(c(p)=1\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} E'_p\geqslant 1\), where one has set

\[ c(p)=\operatorname{codim} (\overline{\{p\}}\cap Z, \overline{\{p\}}). \]

Now if \(p\in U\) and if \(c(p)=1\), denoting by \(\mathfrak{p}\) the ideal of \(A\) corresponding to \(p\), one sees that \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p}=2\), since the complement of \(U\) is a union of a finite number of closed points and \(A\) is a quotient of a regular ring. Moreover, \(E\) is locally free, so, for every \(p\in\operatorname{Supp} E\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} E_p=\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{U, p}\). Finally, if \(p\in U\) and if \(c(p)=1\), one has

\[ \operatorname{prof} E'_p = \operatorname{prof} E_p = \operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{U, p} = \operatorname{prof} A_\mathfrak{p} \geqslant 3-2=1. \]

We must now prove that the natural homomorphism

\[ \tag{1} {\Gamma(U, E) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(\widehat{X}, \widehat{E})} \]

is an isomorphism. Setting then \(\overline{\!E}=u_\ast(E)\), one notes that \(\overline{\!E}\) is coherent and of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at all the closed points of \(X\). It follows that \(\operatorname{R}^i f_\ast(\overline{\!E})\) is coherent for \(i=0, 1\), where \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) denotes the canonical immersion of \(X\) into \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) (Exp. VIII). One then applies (IX 1.5), and one concludes that

\[ \tag{2} \Gamma(U, \overline{\!E}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(\widehat{X}, \widehat{\overline{\!E}}) \]

is an isomorphism, since \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology.

One has a commutative diagram:

\[ \xymatrix@=5mm{ \Gamma(X, \overline{\!E})\ar[rr]^{\simeq}\ar[dr]_{\simeq} && \Gamma(U, E)\ar[dl]\\ &\Gamma(\widehat{X}, \widehat{E})} \]

whence the conclusion.

Let now \(\mathscr{E}\) be a locally free coherent sheaf on \(\widehat{X}\). If one has proved that \(\mathscr{E}\) is algebraizable, i.e. is isomorphic to the formal completion of a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(E\), it is easy to see that \(E\) is locally free in a neighbourhood of \(Y\), hence to prove \(\operatorname{Leff} (X, Y)\). Let \(\widehat{X'}\) be the formal spectrum of \(A\) for the \(t\)-adic topology, which is identified with the formal completion of \(X'\) along \(Y'\). Denote by \(f\) the canonical immersion of \(X\) into \(X'\), by \(f'\) the canonical immersion of \(Y\) into \(Y'\), and by \(\widehat{f}\) the morphism deduced by passage to the completions. For \(\mathscr{E}\) to be algebraizable it suffices that \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathscr{E})\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module, since \(A\) is complete for the t-adic topology. Let \(\mathscr{I} = t\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\), it is an ideal of definition of \(\widehat{X}\).

For every \(n\geqslant 0\), set \(\operatorname{E}_n=\mathscr{E}/\mathscr{I}^{n+1}\). At every closed point \(y\in Y\), the depth of \(\operatorname{E}_0\) is \(\geqslant 2\); indeed \(t\) is an \(A\)-regular element so \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{Y_0, y}=\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, y}-1\geqslant 2\). One concludes that \(\widehat{f}_\ast(\mathscr{E})\) is coherent (IX 2.3). Q.E.D.

Example 2.2

[ Will allow one to compare the fundamental group of a projective variety and of a hyperplane section] Let \(K\) be a field and let \(X\) be a proper \(K\)-prescheme. Let \(\mathscr{L}\) be an ample invertible \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Let \(t \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr{L})\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular element, which means that, for every open set \(U\) and every isomorphism \(u\colon \mathscr{L}~U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_U\), \(u(t)\) is a non-zero-divisor in \(\mathscr{O}_U\) (a condition which does not depend on \(u\)). Let \(Y = V(t)\) be the subscheme of \(X\) with equation \(t = 0\). Then:

  1. If, for every closed point \(x\) in \(X\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\geqslant 2\), one has \(\operatorname{Lef} (X, Y)\);

  2. if moreover, for every closed point \(y\in Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\geqslant 3\), one has \(\operatorname{Leff} (X, Y)\).

This example will be treated in detail in Exp. XII.

Let S be a prescheme, one knows (EGA II 6.1.2) that the functor which, to every finite and flat covering \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), associates the \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebra \(r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)\), induces an equivalence between the category of finite and flat coverings of \(S\) and the category of coherent and locally free \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebras. Let U be an open neighbourhood of \(Y\), and let \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) be a finite and flat covering of U. Let \(\widehat{R}\) be the finite and flat covering of \(\widehat{X}\) deduced from it by base change. One has \(\widehat{r}_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{R}})\simeq\widehat{r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)}\).

Suppose then \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\). This entails that, for every \(U\), the inverse image functor:

\[ \mathbf{L}(U) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{L}(\widehat{X}) \]

is fully faithful. Indeed, let \(E\) and \(F\) be two locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Modules; \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)\) is also coherent and locally free; \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)\) is also coherent and locally free. By hypothesis, the natural map

\[ \Gamma_U (\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_{\widehat{X}}(\widehat{\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)}) \]

is an isomorphism, whence the conclusion, since \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits\) commutes with \(\, \, \widehat{}\, \, \) since everything is locally free. Now the \(\, \, \widehat{}\, \, \) commutes with the tensor product, one deduces that the functor which to every coherent and locally free \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) associates the \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Algebra \(\widehat{\mathscr{A}}\), is fully faithful. Better, if \(E\) is a locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the commutative \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Algebra structures on \(\widehat{E}\).

Proposition 2.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(\widehat{X}\) be the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\). For every open set \(U\) of \(X\), \(U\supset Y\), denote by \(L_U\) (resp. \(P_U\), resp. \(E_U\)) the functor which to every locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module (resp. to every finite and flat covering of \(U\), resp. to every étale covering of \(U\)) associates its inverse image by \(\widehat{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\).

  1. If one has \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\), then for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\), the functors \(L_U\), \(P_U\) and \(E_U\) are fully faithful.

  2. If one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), then for every locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module \(\mathscr{E}\) (resp. ...), there exist an open set \(U\) and a locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module \(E\) (resp. ...), such that \(L_U(E) \simeq \mathscr{E}\) (resp. ...).

(i) Has been seen.

(ii) Follows from (i) and from the hypothesis, at least for \(L_U\) and \(P_U\). Moreover if \(R\) is an étale covering of \(\widehat{X}\), there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\) and a finite and flat covering \(R'\) of \(U\) such that \(\widehat{R'} \simeq R\). One deduces from it a covering \(R''\) of \(Y\) which is étale by 1.1, so \(R'\) is étale in a neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(Y\). Q.E.D.

Corollary 2.4

If one has \(\operatorname{Lef} (X, Y)\), in order that a finite and flat covering \(R\) of an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(R \times_U \widehat{X}\) be so. In particular, in order that \(Y\) be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that the open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) be so, or again that \(X\) be so.

Indeed, in order that a space ringed in local rings \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\Gamma(X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) not be a direct composite of two non-zero rings. Now one has

\[ \Gamma(U, r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)) \simeq \Gamma(\widehat{X}, \widehat{r}_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{R}})) \]

by \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\).

Corollary 2.5

If one has \(\operatorname{Lef} (X, Y)\), then for every \(U\), the functor

\[ \mathbf{Et}(U) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Et}(Y) \]

is fully faithful. If one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), then for every étale covering \(R\) of \(Y\), there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) and a covering \(R'\) of \(U\) such that \(R' \times_U Y \simeq R\).

Corollary 2.6

If one has \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\) and if \(Y\) is connected, every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) is connected and the natural homomorphism \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(U)\) is surjective. If moreover one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), the natural homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_{U}\, \pi_1(U) \]

is an isomorphism. (N.B. One assumes chosen a "base point" in \(Y\), which one also takes as base point in \(X\), for the definition of the fundamental groups.)

All this follows trivially from prop. 1.1 and from prop. 2.3.

blocks.html:

blocks.html:
Example 2.2

[ Will allow one to compare the fundamental group of a projective variety and of a hyperplane section] Let \(K\) be a field and let \(X\) be a proper \(K\)-prescheme. Let \(\mathscr{L}\) be an ample invertible \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Let \(t \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr{L})\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-regular element, which means that, for every open set \(U\) and every isomorphism \(u\colon \mathscr{L}~U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_U\), \(u(t)\) is a non-zero-divisor in \(\mathscr{O}_U\) (a condition which does not depend on \(u\)). Let \(Y = V(t)\) be the subscheme of \(X\) with equation \(t = 0\). Then:

  1. If, for every closed point \(x\) in \(X\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\geqslant 2\), one has \(\operatorname{Lef} (X, Y)\);

  2. if moreover, for every closed point \(y\in Y\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\geqslant 3\), one has \(\operatorname{Leff} (X, Y)\).
This example will be treated in detail in Exp. XII.

Let S be a prescheme, one knows (EGA II 6.1.2) that the functor which, to every finite and flat covering \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\), associates the \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebra \(r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)\), induces an equivalence between the category of finite and flat coverings of \(S\) and the category of coherent and locally free \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebras. Let U be an open neighbourhood of \(Y\), and let \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} U\) be a finite and flat covering of U. Let \(\widehat{R}\) be the finite and flat covering of \(\widehat{X}\) deduced from it by base change. One has \(\widehat{r}_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{R}})\simeq\widehat{r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)}\).
Suppose then \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\). This entails that, for every \(U\), the inverse image functor:
\[ \mathbf{L}(U) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{L}(\widehat{X}) \]

is fully faithful. Indeed, let \(E\) and \(F\) be two locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Modules; \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)\) is also coherent and locally free; \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)\) is also coherent and locally free. By hypothesis, the natural map

\[ \Gamma_U (\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma_{\widehat{X}}(\widehat{\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(E, F)}) \]

is an isomorphism, whence the conclusion, since \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits\) commutes with \(\, \, \widehat{}\, \, \) since everything is locally free. Now the \(\, \, \widehat{}\, \, \) commutes with the tensor product, one deduces that the functor which to every coherent and locally free \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Algebra \(\mathscr{A}\) associates the \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Algebra \(\widehat{\mathscr{A}}\), is fully faithful. Better, if \(E\) is a locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the commutative \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Algebra structures on \(\widehat{E}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Let \(\widehat{X}\) be the formal completion of \(X\) along \(Y\). For every open set \(U\) of \(X\), \(U\supset Y\), denote by \(L_U\) (resp. \(P_U\), resp. \(E_U\)) the functor which to every locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module (resp. to every finite and flat covering of \(U\), resp. to every étale covering of \(U\)) associates its inverse image by \(\widehat{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\).

  1. If one has \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\), then for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\), the functors \(L_U\), \(P_U\) and \(E_U\) are fully faithful.

  2. If one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), then for every locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module \(\mathscr{E}\) (resp. ...), there exist an open set \(U\) and a locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module \(E\) (resp. ...), such that \(L_U(E) \simeq \mathscr{E}\) (resp. ...).
(i) Has been seen.

(ii) Follows from (i) and from the hypothesis, at least for \(L_U\) and \(P_U\). Moreover if \(R\) is an étale covering of \(\widehat{X}\), there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) in \(X\) and a finite and flat covering \(R'\) of \(U\) such that \(\widehat{R'} \simeq R\). One deduces from it a covering \(R''\) of \(Y\) which is étale by 1.1, so \(R'\) is étale in a neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(Y\). Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4

If one has \(\operatorname{Lef} (X, Y)\), in order that a finite and flat covering \(R\) of an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(R \times_U \widehat{X}\) be so. In particular, in order that \(Y\) be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that the open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) be so, or again that \(X\) be so.

Indeed, in order that a space ringed in local rings \((X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\Gamma(X, \mathscr{O}_X)\) not be a direct composite of two non-zero rings. Now one has
\[ \Gamma(U, r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)) \simeq \Gamma(\widehat{X}, \widehat{r}_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{R}})) \]
by \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.5

If one has \(\operatorname{Lef} (X, Y)\), then for every \(U\), the functor

\[ \mathbf{Et}(U) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Et}(Y) \]

is fully faithful. If one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), then for every étale covering \(R\) of \(Y\), there exist an open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) and a covering \(R'\) of \(U\) such that \(R' \times_U Y \simeq R\).

blocks.title: Corollary 2.6
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6

If one has \(\operatorname{Lef}(X, Y)\) and if \(Y\) is connected, every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) is connected and the natural homomorphism \(\pi_1(Y)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(U)\) is surjective. If moreover one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), the natural homomorphism

\[ \pi_1(Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_{U}\, \pi_1(U) \]

is an isomorphism. (N.B. One assumes chosen a "base point" in \(Y\), which one also takes as base point in \(X\), for the definition of the fundamental groups.)
All this follows trivially from prop. 1.1 and from prop. 2.3.

pages.title: Comparison of \(\pi_1(X)\) and \(\pi_1(U)\)
pages.html:
3 Comparison of \(\pi_1(X)\) and \(\pi_1(U)\)

Definition 3.1

Let \(X\) be a prescheme and \(Z\) a closed subset of \(X\). Set \(U=X-Z\). One says that the pair \((X, Z)\) is pure if, for every open set \(V\) of \(X\), the functor

\[ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{Et}(V) &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Et}(V\cap U) \\ V' &\rightsquigarrow V'\times_V(V\cap U) \end{aligned} \]

is an equivalence of categories1.

Definition 3.2

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. Set \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\). Let \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be the radical of \(A\) and let \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be the closed point of \(X\). One says that \(A\) is pure if the pair \((X, \{x\})\) is.

We leave it to the reader not to prove the following proposition:

Proposition 3.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\). In order for the pair \((X, Z)\) to be pure it is necessary and sufficient that, for every \(z\in Z\), the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, z}\) be pure2.

This said, the following theorem is the essential result of this section:

Theorem 3.4

[Purity theorem]

(i) A regular noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 2\) is pure (Zariski-Nagata purity theorem).

(ii) A noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 3\) which is a complete intersection is pure.

Let us recall that a local ring is said to be a complete intersection if there exists a regular noetherian local ring \(B\) and a \(B\)-regular sequence \((t_1, \ldots, t_k)\) of elements of the radical \(\mathfrak{r}(B)\) of \(B\) such that

\[ A \simeq B/(t_1, \ldots, t_k). \]

In this connection let us remark that it would be less ambiguous to say that \(A\) is an absolute complete intersection, as opposed to the situation, which we have already encountered, where \(X\) is a locally noetherian prescheme (which need not be regular) and where \(Y\) is a closed subset of \(X\), which one says is "locally set-theoretically a complete intersection in \(X\)".

Let us first prove a few lemmas.

Lemma 3.5

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(U\) be an open subset of \(X\). Set \(Z=X-U\). Let \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical immersion of \(U\) into \(X\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. For every open set \(V\) of \(X\), if one sets \(V'=V\cap U\), the functor \(F\rightsquigarrow F|{|V'}\) from the category of locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_V\)-Modules to the category of locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{{V'}}\)-Modules is fully faithful;

  2. the natural homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{U})\) is an isomorphism;

  3. for every \(z\in Z\), one has \(\operatorname{prof}\mathscr{O}_{X, z}\geqslant 2\).

We have already seen (III 3.3) the equivalence of (ii) and (iii). Let us show that (ii) implies (i). Let \(F\) and \(G\) be two locally free coherent \(\mathscr{O}_V\)-Modules; \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(F, G)\) is one too, hence \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(F, G)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_\ast(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(F|{|V'}, G|{|V'}))\) is an isomorphism, hence \(\operatorname{Hom}(F, G)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(F|{|V'}, G|{|V'})\). Conversely one takes \(F=G=\mathscr{O}_X\) and applies (i) to every open set \(V\) of \(X\).

Here is a useful "descent lemma":

Lemma 3.6

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Set \(U=X-Z\). Suppose that the homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_\ast(\mathscr{O}_U)\) is an isomorphism. Let \(f\colon X_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism. Set \(Z_1=f^{-1}(Z)\). If the pair \((X_1, Z_1)\) is pure, so is \((X, Z)\).

Let us remark that the hypothesis \(\mathscr{O}_X\simeq i_\ast(\mathscr{O}_U)\) is preserved under flat base extension, since \(i\) is a quasi-compact morphism and, in this case, direct image commutes with inverse image. Now this hypothesis implies that the functor

\[ \mathbf{Et}(V) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Et}(U\cap V) \]

defined by

\[ V'\rightsquigarrow V'\times_V(V\cap U) \]

is fully faithful, as is shown by the interpretation of an étale covering in terms of locally free coherent Algebra. It remains to prove effectivity. One can for example introduce the square \(X_2\) and the cube \(X_3\) of \(X_1\) over \(X\) and one remarks that a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism is a morphism of universal effective descent for the fibred category of étale coverings, over the category of preschemes. The conclusion is formal from there3.

Remark 3.7

We have proved along the way that if \(\mathscr{O}_X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_\ast(\mathscr{O}_U)\) is an isomorphism, \(X\) is connected if and only if \(U\) is, and then \(\pi_1(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(X)\) is surjective.

Corollary 3.8

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring. Suppose that \(\operatorname{prof} A\geqslant 2\). Then if \(\widehat{A}\) is pure, \(A\) is pure.

Follows from lemma 3.5 and lemma 3.6.

The following lemma is the essential point of the proof of the purity theorem:

Lemma 3.9

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring and let \(t\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) be an \(A\)-regular element. Suppose that \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology and moreover a quotient of a regular local ring (for example \(A\) complete). Set \(B=A/tA\).

  1. If for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p}=1\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} A_{\mathfrak{p}}\geqslant 2\), then \(B\) pure implies \(A\) pure.

  2. If for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p}=1\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} A_{\mathfrak{p}}\geqslant 2\), if \(A_\mathfrak{p}\) is pure when \(t\notin\mathfrak{p}\), and \(\operatorname{prof} A_\mathfrak{p}\geqslant3\) when \(t\in\mathfrak{p}\), then \(A\) pure implies \(B\) pure.

Let \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and let \(Y'=V(t)\), which one identifies with the spectrum of \(B\). Let \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\), and set \(X=X'-\{x\}\) and \(Y=Y'-\{x\}=X\cap Y'\). Let us denote by \(X'\) the formal spectrum of \(A\) for the \(t\)-adic topology, which is identified with the formal completion of \(X'\) along \(Y'\).

Since \(A\) is complete for the \(t\)-adic topology, one remarks that \(\mathbf{Et}(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(\widehat{X'})\) is an equivalence of categories. Likewise \(\mathbf{Et}(\widehat{X'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(Y')\) by prop. 1.1, hence \(\mathbf{Et}(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(Y')\) is an equivalence of categories.

Let us show (i). Consider the diagrams

\[ \xymatrix{ X^{\prime} & X\ar[l] && \mathbf{Et}(X^{\prime})\ar[r]^a \ar[d]_c & \mathbf{Et}(X)\ar[d]^b\\ Y^{\prime}\ar[u] & Y\ar[l] \ar[u] && \mathbf{Et}(Y^{\prime})\ar[r]^d & \mathbf{Et}(Y)} \]

We have just seen that \(c\) is an equivalence, \(d\) is one too by the hypothesis that \(B\) is pure, and finally \(b\) is fully faithful as was seen in example 2.1, cf. 2.3 (i).

Let us show (ii). This time one supposes that \(A\) is pure, hence \(a\) is an equivalence; likewise \(c\). Let us see that \(b\) is an equivalence. By example 2.1 one knows that one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), hence \(b\) is already fully faithful; let us prove that it is essentially surjective. One uses 2.3 (ii), noting that, if \(U\) is an open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), the complement of \(U\) in \(X\) is a union of a finite number of closed points; the pair \((X,X-U)\) is therefore pure by prop. 3.3, since at such a point \(\mathfrak{p}\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X,\mathfrak{p}}=A_\mathfrak{p}\) is pure by hypothesis. Whence the conclusion.

Proof of the purity theorem▾

Let us first show (i) by induction on the dimension. Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring of dimension \(2\). Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(X=X'-\{x\}\). One has \(\operatorname{prof} A=2\). One can therefore apply lemma 3.5 to the pair \((X', \{x\})\) and hence \(\mathbf{Et}(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(X)\) is fully faithful. Let now \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be an étale covering defined by a locally free coherent and étale \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebra \(\mathscr{A}=r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)\). Let us denote by \(i\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) the canonical immersion of \(X\) into \(X'\). I claim that \(i_\ast(\mathscr{A})=\mathscr{B}\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Algebra. Indeed, it suffices to apply the "finiteness theorem" VIII 2.3. I claim that this algebra is of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at \(x\). Indeed, it is the direct image of an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, with \(X=X'-\{x\}\). Since \(A\) is a regular ring of dimension \(2\) one has: \(\operatorname{dp} \mathscr{B} + \operatorname{prof}\mathscr{B}=\dim A=2\), where \(\operatorname{dp}\mathscr{B}\) denotes the projective dimension of \(\mathscr{B}\). Hence \(\operatorname{dp}\mathscr{B}=0\), hence \(\mathscr{B}\) is projective, hence free. It follows that \(\mathscr{B}\) defines a finite and flat covering of \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). The set of points of \(X'\) where this covering is not étale is a closed subset of \(X'\) whose equation is a principal ideal: the discriminant ideal of \(\mathscr{B}/A\). Now, by construction, this closed set is contained in \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\), hence is empty since \(\dim A=2\).

Let \(A\) be a regular noetherian local ring, \(\dim A=n\geqslant3\). Suppose (i) proved for rings of dimension \(<n\). To prove that \(A\) is pure, one can suppose \(A\) complete by 3.8. Let \(t\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) whose image in \(\mathfrak{r}(A)/\mathfrak{r}(A)^2\) is nonzero. Then \(B=A/tA\) is a regular noetherian local ring of dimension \(n-1\), hence is pure, since \(n-1\geqslant2\). One concludes by lemma 3.9 (i), which is applicable since \(A\) is complete.

Let us show (ii). Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 3\). Suppose that there exist a regular noetherian local ring \(B\) and a \(B\)-sequence \((t_1, \ldots, t_k)\) such that \(A\simeq B/(t_1, \ldots, t_k)\). Let us show that \(A\) is pure, by induction on \(k\). If \(k=0\), one knows it by (i). Suppose \(k\geqslant1\) and the result established for \(k'<k\). By corollary 3.9 one can suppose that \(A\) (hence also \(B\)) is complete. Set \(C=B/(t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1})\), hence \(A\simeq C/t_kC\) and \(t_k\) is \(C\)-regular. By the induction hypothesis one knows that \(C\) is pure; it suffices to prove that lemma 3.9 (ii) is applicable. Notation: \(A\) and \(B\) of the lemma become \(C\) and \(A\). One has \(\dim C\geqslant4\), hence for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(C\) such that \(\dim C/\mathfrak{p}=1\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} C_\mathfrak{p}\geqslant3\). Moreover, \(C_\mathfrak{p}\) is a complete intersection with \(k'\leqslant k-1\), hence is pure by the induction hypothesis.

Q.E.D.
Theorem 3.10

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Suppose that one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\) (cf. Examples 2.1 and 2.2). Suppose moreover that, for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) and every \(x\in X-U\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(\geqslant2\) or else a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant3\). Then

\[ \pi_0(Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X) \]

is a bijection, and if \(X\) is connected

\[ \pi_1(Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X) \]

is an isomorphism.

There is nothing more to prove. One remarks that, in the two cited examples 2.1 and 2.2, the complement of \(U\) is a union of a finite number of closed points, whence it follows that the hypothesis on the dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is not a hoax.

Let us denote by \(X'\) the formal spectrum of \(A\) for the \(t\)-adic topology, which is identified with the formal completion of \(X'\) along \(Y'\).

Likewise \(\mathbf{Et}(\widehat{X'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(Y')\) by prop. 1.1, hence \(\mathbf{Et}(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(Y')\) is an equivalence of categories.
Let us show (i). Consider the diagrams
\[ \xymatrix{ X^{\prime} & X\ar[l] && \mathbf{Et}(X^{\prime})\ar[r]^a \ar[d]_c & \mathbf{Et}(X)\ar[d]^b\\ Y^{\prime}\ar[u] & Y\ar[l] \ar[u] && \mathbf{Et}(Y^{\prime})\ar[r]^d & \mathbf{Et}(Y)} \]
We have just seen that \(c\) is an equivalence, \(d\) is one too by the hypothesis that \(B\) is pure, and finally \(b\) is fully faithful as was seen in example 2.1, cf. 2.3 (i).

Let us show (ii). This time one supposes that \(A\) is pure, hence \(a\) is an equivalence; likewise \(c\). Let us see that \(b\) is an equivalence. By example 2.1 one knows that one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\), hence \(b\) is already fully faithful; let us prove that it is essentially surjective. One uses 2.3 (ii), noting that, if \(U\) is an open neighbourhood of \(Y\) in \(X\), the complement of \(U\) in \(X\) is a union of a finite number of closed points; the pair \((X,X-U)\) is therefore pure by prop. 3.3, since at such a point \(\mathfrak{p}\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X,\mathfrak{p}}=A_\mathfrak{p}\) is pure by hypothesis. Whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Proof of the purity theorem
blocks.html:
Proof of the purity theorem▾

Let us first show (i) by induction on the dimension. Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring of dimension \(2\). Set \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\), \(X=X'-\{x\}\). One has \(\operatorname{prof} A=2\). One can therefore apply lemma 3.5 to the pair \((X', \{x\})\) and hence \(\mathbf{Et}(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathbf{Et}(X)\) is fully faithful. Let now \(r\colon R\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be an étale covering defined by a locally free coherent and étale \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebra \(\mathscr{A}=r_\ast(\mathscr{O}_R)\). Let us denote by \(i\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) the canonical immersion of \(X\) into \(X'\). I claim that \(i_\ast(\mathscr{A})=\mathscr{B}\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\)-Algebra. Indeed, it suffices to apply the "finiteness theorem" VIII 2.3. I claim that this algebra is of depth \(\geqslant 2\) at \(x\). Indeed, it is the direct image of an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, with \(X=X'-\{x\}\). Since \(A\) is a regular ring of dimension \(2\) one has: \(\operatorname{dp} \mathscr{B} + \operatorname{prof}\mathscr{B}=\dim A=2\), where \(\operatorname{dp}\mathscr{B}\) denotes the projective dimension of \(\mathscr{B}\). Hence \(\operatorname{dp}\mathscr{B}=0\), hence \(\mathscr{B}\) is projective, hence free. It follows that \(\mathscr{B}\) defines a finite and flat covering of \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). The set of points of \(X'\) where this covering is not étale is a closed subset of \(X'\) whose equation is a principal ideal: the discriminant ideal of \(\mathscr{B}/A\). Now, by construction, this closed set is contained in \(x=\mathfrak{r}(A)\), hence is empty since \(\dim A=2\).

Let \(A\) be a regular noetherian local ring, \(\dim A=n\geqslant3\). Suppose (i) proved for rings of dimension \(<n\). To prove that \(A\) is pure, one can suppose \(A\) complete by 3.8. Let \(t\in\mathfrak{r}(A)\) whose image in \(\mathfrak{r}(A)/\mathfrak{r}(A)^2\) is nonzero. Then \(B=A/tA\) is a regular noetherian local ring of dimension \(n-1\), hence is pure, since \(n-1\geqslant2\). One concludes by lemma 3.9 (i), which is applicable since \(A\) is complete.

Let us show (ii). Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geqslant 3\). Suppose that there exist a regular noetherian local ring \(B\) and a \(B\)-sequence \((t_1, \ldots, t_k)\) such that \(A\simeq B/(t_1, \ldots, t_k)\). Let us show that \(A\) is pure, by induction on \(k\). If \(k=0\), one knows it by (i). Suppose \(k\geqslant1\) and the result established for \(k'<k\). By corollary 3.9 one can suppose that \(A\) (hence also \(B\)) is complete. Set \(C=B/(t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1})\), hence \(A\simeq C/t_kC\) and \(t_k\) is \(C\)-regular. By the induction hypothesis one knows that \(C\) is pure; it suffices to prove that lemma 3.9 (ii) is applicable. Notation: \(A\) and \(B\) of the lemma become \(C\) and \(A\). One has \(\dim C\geqslant4\), hence for every prime ideal \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(C\) such that \(\dim C/\mathfrak{p}=1\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} C_\mathfrak{p}\geqslant3\). Moreover, \(C_\mathfrak{p}\) is a complete intersection with \(k'\leqslant k-1\), hence is pure by the induction hypothesis.

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.10

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed subset of \(X\). Suppose that one has \(\operatorname{Leff}(X, Y)\) (cf. Examples 2.1 and 2.2). Suppose moreover that, for every open neighbourhood \(U\) of \(Y\) and every \(x\in X-U\), the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(\geqslant2\) or else a complete intersection of dimension \(\geqslant3\). Then

\[ \pi_0(Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_0(X) \]

is a bijection, and if \(X\) is connected

\[ \pi_1(Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(X) \]

is an isomorphism.

There is nothing more to prove. One remarks that, in the two cited examples 2.1 and 2.2, the complement of \(U\) is a union of a finite number of closed points, whence it follows that the hypothesis on the dimension of \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is not a hoax.

footnotes.html: For a notion more satisfactory in certain respects, cf. the commentary XIV 1.6 d).
Compare the non-commutative case of XIV 1.8, whose proof is essentially the same as that of 3.3.
Cf. J. Giraud, Méthode de la descente, Mémoire nº 2 du Bulletin de la Société Mathématique de France (1964).
